96:   - Summary:
97:     Dylan owns a fork of [sst/opencode](https://github.com/sst/opencode) at `~/Documents/personal/opencode`. The fork is 16 custom commits ahead of upstream (linear forward — all upstream changes included). Custom changes focus on **memory management** (LRU/TTL instance eviction preventing 8.4GB unbounded growth), **SSE cleanup** (idempotent teardown preventing leaked connections), **OAuth stealth mode** (Claude Max access), **ORCH_WORKER header forwarding** (worker session detection), **session metadata** (extensible key-value storage), and **session TTL** (auto-expiry with periodic cleanup). The fork is a TypeScript monorepo (Bun runtime, Hono HTTP framework) with sessions stored in SQLite database (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db) using Drizzle ORM with migration-based schema management. Session status (idle/busy/retry) is tracked in-memory only, lost on server restart. A `GET /session/status` endpoint already exists for querying session state.

108:   - Summary:
109:     The Coaching Plugin is an OpenCode plugin that provides real-time behavioral feedback to orchestrators and workers through tool usage pattern detection. It implements the "Pain as Signal" architectural pattern: agents should feel friction in real-time rather than learning about it post-hoc.
110:     
111:     The plugin hooks `tool.execute.after` to observe tool usage patterns (it cannot see LLM response text—fundamental constraint), detects 8 behavioral patterns using behavioral proxies (action ratio, analysis paralysis, frame collapse, etc.), and injects coaching messages via `client.session.prompt({ noReply: true })`. Metrics persist to `~/.orch/coaching-metrics.jsonl` and are exposed via `/api/coaching` for dashboard visualization.
112:     
113:     **Current status (Feb 2026):** Both orchestrator and worker coaching operational. Orchestrator coaching: 1000+ metrics collected. Worker health tracking: verified working Feb 14 — stress test (50+ tool calls) emitted `context_usage` worker metric with zero orchestrator metric leakage. Fix required two opencode fork commits (459a1bfba, 0922edfe7) to: (1) read `x-opencode-env-ORCH_WORKER` header and set `session.metadata.role='worker'`, (2) pass `session.metadata` through `tool.execute.after` plugin hooks. **Note:** Worker detection only works for headless (OpenCode HTTP API) spawns — Claude CLI/tmux spawns bypass the HTTP session creation endpoint and don't get metadata set.
114:     
115:     ---
116:   - Critical Invariants:
117:     1. **Plugins cannot see LLM response text** - Only tool calls visible, not free-text responses. Fundamental constraint, not fixable.
118:     2. **Behavioral proxies are the only detection mechanism** - All pattern detection uses tool usage as proxy signals.
119:     3. **Metrics persist, session state doesn't** - JSONL file survives restarts, in-memory Map is ephemeral.
120:     4. **Observation coupled to intervention** - Injection only fires from `flushMetrics` within `tool.execute.after` hook.
121:     5. **Worker detection caching is one-way** - Only cache `true` results (confirmed worker), never cache `false`.
122:     6. **Two injection mechanisms serve different purposes** - `config.instructions` adds file references at config time (static context like skills), `client.session.prompt(noReply: true)` injects content at runtime (immediate coaching).
123:     
124:     ---
125:   - Why This Fails:
126:     ### Failure Mode 1: Worker Detection Caching Bug (Jan 10-17)
127:     
128:     **Symptom:** Zero worker health metrics in production despite implemented code
129:     
130:     **Root cause:** `detectWorkerSession()` cached both `true` AND `false` results, permanently misclassifying workers if ANY tool call happened before a worker-identifying signal
131:     
132:     **Why it happens:**
133:     1. Worker session starts, first tool = `glob` (no detection signal)
134:     2. `isWorker = false` → cached → function returns `false` forever
135:     3. Second tool = `read SPAWN_CONTEXT.md` → cached result returned, detection skipped
136:     4. Worker treated as orchestrator for entire session
137:     
138:     **Cascade:**
139:     ```
140:     First non-matching tool call → cache false → subsequent detection signals ignored → worker health code never runs
141:     ```
142:     
143:     **Fix (Jan 17):** Only cache `true` results (confirmed worker), never cache `false`. Allow re-evaluation on each tool call until worker confirmed.
144:     
145:     **Pattern established:** "Never cache negative results in per-session detection"
146:     
147:     ---
148:     
149:     ### Failure Mode 2: Invalid Detection Signal (Bash workdir)
150:     
151:     **Symptom:** Detection code exists but never fires
152:     
153:     **Root cause:** Detection checked for `args.workdir` on bash tool, but bash tool has no `workdir` argument
154:     
155:     **Why it happens:**
156:     - Bash tool args are: `command`, `timeout`, `dangerouslyDisableSandbox`, `run_in_background`
157:     - No `workdir` argument exists
158:     - Detection signal `if (tool === "bash" && args?.workdir)` never matches
159:     
160:     **Fix (Jan 17):** Removed broken detection signal, restored file-path detection for any `.orch/workspace/` path
161:     
162:     ---
163:     
164:     ### Failure Mode 3: Observation Coupled to Intervention (Restart Brittleness)
165:     
166:     **Symptom:** Coaching messages stop after OpenCode server restart, even though metrics show problems
167:     
168:     **Root cause:** Injection is implemented as side effect of metric collection, not as separate concern that can operate independently
169:     
170:     **Why it happens:**
171:     - Metrics are **persistent** (JSONL file, survive restart)
172:     - Session state is **in-memory** (Map, lost on restart)
173:     - Injection logic **coupled to metric collection** (only happens via `flushMetrics`)
174:     - After restart: metrics file shows "poor" status, but no session state exists, so injection never fires
175:     
176:     **Cascade:**
177:     ```
178:     Server restart → session state lost → flushMetrics not called → injection doesn't fire → coaching stops
179:     ```
180:     
181:     **Architectural fix (not yet implemented):** Separate injection into independent daemon that reads metrics from JSONL and injects via OpenCode API, completely decoupled

197:   - Summary:
198:     The Swarm Dashboard is a Svelte 5 web UI served by `orch serve` (Go backend) that provides real-time monitoring of agent status, daemon health, and operational metrics.
199:     
200:     **Critical context (Option A+):** The dashboard is Dylan's (meta-orchestrator's) ONLY observability layer. He does not use CLI tools directly. Dashboard failure = Dylan is blind. This makes dashboard reliability tier-0 infrastructure. See orchestrator skill "Observability Architecture (Option A+)" section.
201:     
202:     The architecture uses a **two-mode design** (Operational/Historical) to separate daily coordination from deep analysis. SSE connections enable real-time updates but are constrained by HTTP/1.1's 6-connection limit. Progressive disclosure and stable sorting prevent information overload while maintaining scan-ability.
203:     
204:     ---
205:   - Critical Invariants:
206:     1. **Two-mode design is mutually exclusive** - Cannot show both Operational and Historical views simultaneously
207:     2. **SSE Events auto-connect, Agentlog is opt-in** - Connection pool management
208:     3. **beadsFetchThreshold controls remote queries** - 5+ ready issues triggers `bd ready` shell-out
209:     4. **Progressive disclosure via collapsed panels** - Event panels start collapsed, expand on click
210:     5. **Stable sort maintains scan-ability** - Agent order doesn't change unless status changes
211:     6. **Early filtering reduces payload size** - Backend filters before sending to frontend
212:     
213:     ---
214:   - Why This Fails:
215:     ### Failure Mode 1: Connection Pool Exhaustion
216:     
217:     **Symptom:** API fetches hang or timeout when SSE panels open
218:     
219:     **Root cause:** HTTP/1.1 allows only 6 connections per origin; SSE occupies slots
220:     
221:     **Why it happens:**
222:     - Events SSE (auto-connect): 1 slot
223:     - Agentlog SSE (auto-connect before fix): 1 slot
224:     - Remaining 4 slots for API fetches
225:     - If 5+ API requests concurrent, some block
226:     
227:     **Fix (Jan 5):** Made Agentlog SSE opt-in via Follow button, freeing 1 slot
228:     
229:     ### Failure Mode 2: Slow Dashboard Load with 100+ Agents
230:     
231:     **Symptom:** Dashboard takes 5-10 seconds to load with many agents
232:     
233:     **Root cause:** `/api/agents` endpoint performs expensive operations (OpenCode queries, beads parsing) synchronously
234:     
235:     **Why it happens:**
236:     - Each agent requires OpenCode session query
237:     - Full beads issue parsing for each agent
238:     - No caching, recomputed on every request
239:     
240:     **Fix (Jan 6):** Response caching with 2-second TTL, reduced load time to <1 second
241:     
242:     ### Failure Mode 3: Information Overload in Operational Mode
243:     
244:     **Symptom:** Users overwhelmed by full swarm map with 50+ agents
245:     
246:     **Root cause:** Single view tried to serve both daily coordination and deep analysis
247:     
248:     **Why it happens:**
249:     - Operational needs: "What's ready? What's broken?"
250:     - Historical needs: "Show me everything, all filters, full archive"
251:     - One view can't optimize for both
252:     
253:     **Fix (Jan 7):** Two-mode design - Operational (focused) vs Historical (comprehensive)
254:     
255:     ### Failure Mode 4: Plugin Cascade (Dashboard "Disconnected" Despite Services Running)
256:     
257:     **Symptom:** Dashboard shows "disconnected", `overmind status` shows all 3 services running, but `orch status` returns HTTP 500
258:     
259:     **Root cause:** OpenCode plugin error (e.g., v1→v2 API incompatibility) crashes OpenCode's internal request handling
260:     
261:     **Why it happens:**
262:     - OpenCode loads plugins at startup
263:     - Bad plugin throws error on every request
264:     - `/api/agents` calls OpenCode → gets 500
265:     - Dashboard can't fetch agent data → shows "disconnected"
266:     - overmind sees process running (not crashed) → reports "running"
267:     
268:     **Cascade:**
269:     ```
270:     Plugin error → OpenCode internal 500 → orch status fails → API can't get agents → Dashboard "disconnected"
271:     ```
272:     
273:     **Fix (Jan 14):** Disable plugins, restart OpenCode, re-enable one-by-one. Root cause was session-resume.js using v1 API (object export) instead of v2 (function export).
274:     
275:     **Key insight:** Dashboard can appear "down" while all processes are technically "running". Health checks must verify data flow, not just port availability.
276:

289:   - Summary:
290:     Active OpenCode sessions can become unfindable through **7 independent vectors** spanning 3 systems (disk-cleanup.sh, orch-go cleanup, OpenCode itself). The fundamental problem is that no "session is active, do not touch" lock exists, and multiple processes can delete sessions from the shared SQLite database without coordination. The Ctrl+D keybind is triple-bound (app exit, session delete, input delete), creating the highest-risk accidental deletion path. The disk-cleanup.sh vector was the first confirmed root cause (now fixed), but the bug persists because at least two other vectors remain open.
291:     
292:     ---
293:   - Critical Invariants:
294:     1. **Sessions exist in SQLite or they don't** - There is no "evicted but recoverable" state
295:     2. **NotFoundError = row deleted from DB** - Not a caching issue
296:     3. **Multiple processes share one SQLite DB** - No coordination protocol
297:     4. **Cascade deletes propagate silently** - Deleting a session kills all messages and parts
298:     5. **No "active session" lock exists** - Any process can delete any session at any time
299:     6. **JSON→SQLite migration is one-time** - Gate checks `opencode.db` existence, not whether sessions were imported. DB existed from Jan 27 schema migrations → 188 JSON sessions permanently orphaned, invisible to current code
300:     
301:     ---
302:   - Why This Fails:
303:     ### Failure Mode 1: Untracked Session Deletion (Vector #2)
304:     
305:     **Symptom:** Interactive TUI session disappears mid-conversation with NotFoundError
306:     
307:     **Root cause:** `cleanUntrackedDiskSessions()` in `clean_cmd.go:408-539` finds sessions not tracked by any `.orch/workspace/*/session_id` file, checks if they were updated in the last 5 minutes, and deletes them if idle.
308:     
309:     **Why TUI sessions are vulnerable:**
310:     - Interactive/orchestrator TUI sessions have NO workspace directory
311:     - No `.session_id` file means Layer 1 protection is bypassed entirely
312:     - If the user hasn't sent a message in >5 minutes (reading, thinking, context switch), Layer 2 (recency) marks it as orphaned
313:     - Layer 3 (`IsSessionProcessing()`) only runs for recently-active sessions
314:     - Session gets deleted via `client.DeleteSession(session.ID)` at line 525
315:     
316:     **Code path:**
317:     ```
318:     orch clean --sessions (or --all)
319:       → cleanUntrackedDiskSessions()
320:         → !trackedSessionIDs[session.ID]  ← TUI has no workspace, always true
321:         → now.Sub(updatedAt) > 5min       ← User paused, true
322:         → (skips IsSessionProcessing)     ← Only checked for recent sessions
323:         → client.DeleteSession(session.ID) ← SESSION DELETED
324:     ```
325:     
326:     **Fix needed:** Call `IsSessionProcessing()` for ALL untracked sessions, not just recently active ones. Cost: one API call per untracked session.
327:     
328:     ### Failure Mode 2: Accidental Ctrl+D Deletion (Vector #3)
329:     
330:     **Symptom:** Session vanishes after user presses Ctrl+D
331:     
332:     **Root cause:** Three keybinds share `ctrl+d`:
333:     - `app_exit: "ctrl+c,ctrl+d,<leader>q"` (config.ts:771)
334:     - `session_delete: "ctrl+d"` (config.ts:784)
335:     - `input_delete: "ctrl+d,delete,shift+delete"` (config.ts:878)
336:     
337:     **Why it happens:**
338:     1. User opens session list (`<leader>l`)
339:     2. User wants to exit the list, presses Ctrl+D (habit from terminal/vim)
340:     3. Session list dialog intercepts as `session_delete`, highlights session in red
341:     4. If user presses Ctrl+D again (common stutter or habit), session is permanently deleted
342:     5. TUI crashes with NotFoundError on next render cycle
343:     
344:     The confirmation ("Press ctrl+d again to confirm") is displayed as red-highlighted title text that may not be noticed in a fast interaction.
345:     
346:     **Fix needed:** Rebind `session_delete` to a non-conflicting key, or add a modal confirmation dialog.
347:     
348:     ### Failure Mode 3: External Process Deletion (Vector #4)
349:     
350:     **Symptom:** Session disappears without user action
351:     
352:     **Root cause:** `DELETE /session/:id` route has no authentication and no coordination. Any local process can delete

462:   - Summary:
463:     Beads integration uses **RPC-first with CLI fallback** pattern: try native Go RPC client (fast, no process spawn), fall back to CLI subprocess if daemon unavailable. The integration operates at **three points in agent lifecycle**: spawn (create issue), work (report phase via comments), complete (close with reason). **Auto-tracking** creates issues automatically unless `--no-track` flag set. The RPC client lives in **pkg/beads** (never shell out with `exec.Command` directly) and provides 10x performance improvement over CLI (single RPC call vs subprocess spawn + JSON parse).

466:   - Why This Fails:
467:     ### 1. RPC Client Unavailable
468:     
469:     **What happens:** RPC calls fail, client falls back to CLI, performance degrades.
470:     
471:     **Root cause:** Beads daemon not running. RPC socket `~/.beads/daemon.sock` doesn't exist.
472:     
473:     **Why detection is hard:** Fallback is silent. No warning that RPC failed. User sees slow performance, doesn't know why.
474:     
475:     **Fix:** Start beads daemon: `bd daemon start` or ensure launchd starts it on boot.
476:     
477:     **Detection:** Log RPC failures, surface in `orch doctor` health check.
478:     
479:     ### 2. Beads ID Not Found
480:     
481:     **What happens:** `orch complete orch-go-abc1` fails with "issue not found".
482:     
483:     **Root cause:** Cross-project spawn. Issue created in orch-knowledge, but trying to complete from orch-go. Beads scoped to current directory's `.beads/`.
484:     
485:     **Why detection is hard:** Beads ID looks valid (correct format), but doesn't exist in current project's `.beads/issues.jsonl`.
486:     
487:     **Fix:** `cd` into correct project before completion, or use `--workdir` flag.
488:     
489:     **Prevention:** `orch complete` should detect project from workspace, auto-cd.
490:     
491:     ### 3. Auto-Tracking Creates Duplicates
492:     
493:     **What happens:** `orch spawn` creates issue, but issue already exists for same work.
494:     
495:     **Root cause:** User creates issue manually, then spawns with auto-tracking. Both create issue.
496:     
497:     **Why detection is hard:** No deduplication. Beads doesn't check if similar issue exists.
498:     
499:     **Fix:** Use `--issue <id>` flag to reference existing issue instead of auto-creating.
500:     
501:     **Prevention:** Better UX: `orch spawn` could check for related issues and prompt user.

Perfect! All tasks completed successfully:

## Summary

I've successfully wired the attention store to the `/api/attention` endpoint, replacing the stub implementation with a full integration:

### Changes Made

1. **Updated store structure** - Now holds both `completedIssues: CompletedIssue[]` and `signals: Map<string, AttentionSignal>`

2. **Implemented real fetch()** - Calls `GET /api/attention?project={projectDir}` and transforms the response

3. **Signal to badge mapping** - Correctly maps attention signals to badge types:
   - `recently-closed` + `unverified` → `verify`
   - `recently-closed` + `verified` → `recently_closed`
   - `recently-closed` + `needs_fix` → `verify_failed`
   - `likely-done` → `likely_done`
   - `stuck` → `stuck`
   - `unblocked` → `unblocked`

4. **Updated ATTENTION_BADGE_CONFIG** - Added all 9 badge types with colors (verify, decide, escalate, likely_done, recently_closed, unblocked, stuck, crashed, verify_failed)

5. **Preserved exports** - Kept `formatRelativeTime()` and `ATTENTION_BADGE_CONFIG` as required

### Verification

✅ Build succeeds: `npm run build` completed without errors
✅ Type safety: All TypeScript types properly defined
✅ Integration: Work-graph page already calls `attention.fetch(projectDir)`

The attention store is now fully functional and ready to display rich attention signals on the work-graph dashboard.
